text: The patient is positioned in a modified lithotomy position with a Foley catheter inserted for bladder decompression. A 10mm port is placed below the umbilicus, and pneumoperitoneum is established. The patient is then positioned in Trendelenburg. Four additional 5mm ports are inserted in the left upper, left lower, right upper, and right lower quadrants under direct vision. The surgeon inspects the abdomen and sweeps the small bowel out of the pelvis.

The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon cephalad and laterally, exposing the sacral promontory. The peritoneum is scored using electrocautery or an advanced energy device, while avoiding large sacral veins and low crossing iliac vessels. Dissection continues down the right side of the rectum toward the pelvic floor, taking care to preserve the lateral ligaments and hypogastric plexus.

The surgeon dissects anteriorly onto the rectum a few centimeters above the rectovaginal fold. A pre-cut piece of biologic mesh, measuring four by twelve centimeters, is introduced through the ten millimeter port. The mesh is secured to the anterior wall of the distal rectum using four to six interrupted polypropylene sutures, taking only seromuscular bites. A digital rectal exam is performed to ensure the sutures are not full thickness and are positioned above the sphincter complex.

The surgeon gently pulls the mesh cephalad and identifies a suitable area on the sacral promontory for mesh placement to reduce the rectum without excess tension. Two 2-0 braided polyester sutures are placed from the presacral fascia to the proximal end of the mesh. A sliding knot is tied extracorporeally to secure the mesh to the sacral promontory. The peritoneum is closed over the entire mesh using a 2-0 barbed suture, starting at the sacral promontory.
The surgeon closes the fascia of the ten millimeter port under direct vision and subsequently removes the remaining ports from the abdominal wall.
